comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around a physical implementation of Conway's Game of Life, with commenters appreciating the scale, design, and nostalgic or artistic aspects. Some share personal experiences or ideas for similar projects, while a few critique the concept of a physical versus digital version.

comments.author: possiblydrunk
comments.text: Nicely done! Scale matters.  If you make something big enough relative to its expected size, it will impress and captivate, even if it's simple. General observation, not that the construction here was by any means simple.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully highlights the importance of scale in making simple projects impressive, a valid observation supported by design principles.

comments.author: eps
comments.text: I saw one in a computer museum in Switzerland. It was a much larger field, it was just large orange LEDs (or were they tubes?), but it also cycled between a dozen of different cell automata games. Something about being able to see individual "pixels" made it really mesmerizing.
comments.analysis: The comment provides a relevant real-world example of a similar installation, adding context and credibility to the discussion.

comments.author: cjfd
comments.text: When I was a teenager, I read a book about assembly language for the commodore and implemented the game of life in a really simple way. I just used the text screen. To switch on a cell, I would put an asterisk ('*') in it. Then I could run my machine code program and it would evolve according to the rules of the game of life.
comments.analysis: This personal anecdote is plausible and adds a nostalgic perspective on implementing the Game of Life, enriching the conversation.

comments.author: self_awareness
comments.text: That's not a "physical" version of game of life -- that's a digital version, like every version, but with bigger pixels.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly distinguishes between physical and digital implementations, offering a precise technical critique.

comments.author: Cthulhu_
comments.text: I love this and would love to see it on a wall at our office or something like that. Maybe there's smaller/cheaper led/switches that would work in a handheld version.
comments.analysis: The comment expresses enthusiasm and suggests practical improvements, contributing constructively to the discussion.

comments.author: PetitPrince
comments.text: My Alma matter has a jumbo version of this, in which the game if life is one of several available mode https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BioWall
comments.analysis: This comment provides a factual reference to a similar large-scale installation, adding informative value.

comments.author: slow_typist
comments.text:
Très cool.

A grid of capacitive touch sensors could be printed directly on the pcb, bringing down costs by a degree of magnitude. Real switches are much more satisfying though.
comments.analysis: The comment offers a knowledgeable suggestion for cost reduction while acknowledging user experience, showing balanced insight.

comments.author: mastermedo
comments.text: A thousand bucks for 17x17 touchscreen. Add a painting frame, hang it on the wall, and you made yourself amazing art for cheap.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a reasonable cost estimate and an artistic perspective, which is plausible and relevant.

comments.author: Traubenfuchs
comments.text:
Would be interesting to do this with people and observe the inevitable mistakes they make.

Now that would be simulating life witg life.
comments.analysis: The comment creatively extends the concept metaphorically, though it is speculative and less concrete.

comments.author: exolab
comments.text:
> I figured out what would be a reasonable amount to spend on the project and then multiplied that by 10.

I like the way you think.
comments.analysis: This comment is more of a humorous or emotional reaction than a substantive contribution.
